​
Chrome Extension
Monitors XHR requests/responses and console logs
Tracks selected DOM elements
Sends all logs and current element to the BrowserKit Connector
Connects to Websocket server to capture/send screenshots
Allows user to configure token/truncation limits + screenshot folder path
​
Node Server
Acts as middleware between the Chrome extension and MCP server
Receives logs and currently selected element from Chrome extension
Processes requests from MCP server to capture logs, screenshot or current element
Sends Websocket command to the Chrome extension for capturing a screenshot
Intelligently truncates strings and # of duplicate objects in logs to avoid token limits
Removes cookies and sensitive headers to avoid sending to LLMs in MCP clients
​
MCP Server
Implements the Model Context Protocol
Provides standardized tools for AI clients
Compatible with various MCP clients (Cursor, Cline, Zed, Claude Desktop, etc.)


Get Started
Quickstart
Start using BrowserTools MCP

If you have not already configured BrowserToolsMCP, make sure to visit the installation page to get everything set up!

With BrowsersToolsMCP now installed and configured with your MCP client, you can enable your favorite AI code editors and agents to have access to the following capabilities:


​
Analyzing Logs for Debugging & Validation
Get Console Logs: Returns the most recent console logs

Get Console Errors: Returns the most recent console errors

Get XHR Network Logs: Returns all of the most recent xhr requests that results in an HTTP success response code like 200 or 300

Get XHR Network Errors: Returns the most recent xhr requests that returned a 400 or 500 HTTP error response code

“Can you check console and network logs to see what went wrong?”
Cursor agent will automatically use all four tools if you ask this when debugging.

Wipe Logs: Deletes all logs stored on the server

“Wipe the logs”
Cursor agent will delete all logs stored in the server so that you have a fresh slate for debugging. Useful if you don’t want to refresh to browser to wipe reset/wipe your logs.

​
Updating & Debugging UI Components
Capture Screenshot: Sends a screenshot to /user/Downloads/mcp-screenshots

“Something doesn’t look right in the UI. Can you take a screenshot?”

We suggest setting a custom path in the dev tools panel so screenshots are sent directly to AI code editors workspace / current project.

Then you can easily drag n’ drop screenshots into your AI chat interface.

Get currently selected element: Returns the currently selected element.

”Can you edit the currently selected element to do x, y and z?”
When using the chrome dev tools you can use the Element Selector tool to select any component in your UI.

After selecting an element, you’ll be be able to ask Cursor to edit or find the currently selected element in your code. This makes traversing through a codebase and making UI changes much easier.


BrowserTools MCP
Make your AI tools 10x more aware and capable of interacting with your browser

This application is a powerful browser monitoring and interaction tool that enables AI-powered applications via Anthropic's Model Context Protocol (MCP) to capture and analyze browser data through a Chrome extension.

Read our docs for the full installation, quickstart and contribution guides.

Roadmap
Check out our project roadmap here: Github Roadmap / Project Board

Updates
v1.2.0 is out! Here's a quick breakdown of the update:

You can now enable "Allow Auto-Paste into Cursor" within the DevTools panel. Screenshots will be automatically pasted into Cursor (just make sure to focus/click into the Agent input field in Cursor, otherwise it won't work!)
Integrated a suite of SEO, performance, accessibility, and best practice analysis tools via Lighthouse
Implemented a NextJS specific prompt used to improve SEO for a NextJS application
Added Debugger Mode as a tool which executes all debugging tools in a particular sequence, along with a prompt to improve reasoning
Added Audit Mode as a tool to execute all auditing tools in a particular sequence
Resolved Windows connectivity issues
Improved networking between BrowserTools server, extension and MCP server with host/port auto-discovery, auto-reconnect, and graceful shutdown mechanisms
Added ability to more easily exit out of the Browser Tools server with Ctrl+C
Quickstart Guide
There are three components to run this MCP tool:

Install our chrome extension from here: v1.2.0 BrowserToolsMCP Chrome Extension
Install the MCP server from this command within your IDE: npx @agentdeskai/browser-tools-mcp@latest
Open a new terminal and run this command: npx @agentdeskai/browser-tools-server@latest
Different IDEs have different configs but this command is generally a good starting point; please reference your IDEs docs for the proper config setup
IMPORTANT TIP - there are two servers you need to install. There's...

browser-tools-server (local nodejs server that's a middleware for gathering logs) and
browser-tools-mcp (MCP server that you install into your IDE that communicates w/ the extension + browser-tools-server)
npx @agentdeskai/browser-tools-mcp@latest is what you put into your IDE npx @agentdeskai/browser-tools-server@latest is what you run in a new terminal window

After those three steps, open up your chrome dev tools and then the BrowserToolsMCP panel.

If you're still having issues try these steps:

Quit / close down your browser. Not just the window but all of Chrome itself.
Restart the local node server (browser-tools-server)
Make sure you only have ONE instance of chrome dev tools panel open
After that, it should work but if it doesn't let me know and I can share some more steps to gather logs/info about the issue!

If you have any questions or issues, feel free to open an issue ticket! And if you have any ideas to make this better, feel free to reach out or open an issue ticket with an enhancement tag or reach out to me at @tedx_ai on x

Full Update Notes:
Coding agents like Cursor can run these audits against the current page seamlessly. By leveraging Puppeteer and the Lighthouse npm library, BrowserTools MCP can now:

Evaluate pages for WCAG compliance
Identify performance bottlenecks
Flag on-page SEO issues
Check adherence to web development best practices
Review NextJS specific issues with SEO
...all without leaving your IDE 🎉

🔑 Key Additions
Audit Type	Description
Accessibility	WCAG-compliant checks for color contrast, missing alt text, keyboard navigation traps, ARIA attributes, and more.
Performance	Lighthouse-driven analysis of render-blocking resources, excessive DOM size, unoptimized images, and other factors affecting page speed.
SEO	Evaluates on-page SEO factors (like metadata, headings, and link structure) and suggests improvements for better search visibility.
Best Practices	Checks for general best practices in web development.
NextJS Audit	Injects a prompt used to perform a NextJS audit.
Audit Mode	Runs all auditing tools in a sequence.
Debugger Mode	Runs all debugging tools in a sequence.
🛠️ Using Audit Tools
✅ Before You Start
Ensure you have:

An active tab in your browser
The BrowserTools extension enabled
▶️ Running Audits
Headless Browser Automation:
Puppeteer automates a headless Chrome instance to load the page and collect audit data, ensuring accurate results even for SPAs or content loaded via JavaScript.

The headless browser instance remains active for 60 seconds after the last audit call to efficiently handle consecutive audit requests.

Structured Results:
Each audit returns results in a structured JSON format, including overall scores and detailed issue lists. This makes it easy for MCP-compatible clients to interpret the findings and present actionable insights.

The MCP server provides tools to run audits on the current page. Here are example queries you can use to trigger them:

Accessibility Audit (runAccessibilityAudit)
Ensures the page meets accessibility standards like WCAG.

Example Queries:

"Are there any accessibility issues on this page?"
"Run an accessibility audit."
"Check if this page meets WCAG standards."
Performance Audit (runPerformanceAudit)
Identifies performance bottlenecks and loading issues.

Example Queries:

"Why is this page loading so slowly?"
"Check the performance of this page."
"Run a performance audit."
SEO Audit (runSEOAudit)
Evaluates how well the page is optimized for search engines.

Example Queries:

"How can I improve SEO for this page?"
"Run an SEO audit."
"Check SEO on this page."
Best Practices Audit (runBestPracticesAudit)
Checks for general best practices in web development.

Example Queries:

"Run a best practices audit."
"Check best practices on this page."
"Are there any best practices issues on this page?"
Audit Mode (runAuditMode)
Runs all audits in a particular sequence. Will run a NextJS audit if the framework is detected.

Example Queries:

"Run audit mode."
"Enter audit mode."
NextJS Audits (runNextJSAudit)
Checks for best practices and SEO improvements for NextJS applications

Example Queries:

"Run a NextJS audit."
"Run a NextJS audit, I'm using app router."
"Run a NextJS audit, I'm using page router."
Debugger Mode (runDebuggerMode)
Runs all debugging tools in a particular sequence

Example Queries:

"Enter debugger mode."
Architecture
There are three core components all used to capture and analyze browser data:

Chrome Extension: A browser extension that captures screenshots, console logs, network activity and DOM elements.
Node Server: An intermediary server that facilitates communication between the Chrome extension and any instance of an MCP server.
MCP Server: A Model Context Protocol server that provides standardized tools for AI clients to interact with the browser.
┌─────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐     ┌───────────────┐     ┌─────────────┐
│  MCP Client │ ──► │  MCP Server  │ ──► │  Node Server  │ ──► │   Chrome    │
│  (e.g.      │ ◄── │  (Protocol   │ ◄── │ (Middleware)  │ ◄── │  Extension  │
│   Cursor)   │     │   Handler)   │     │               │     │             │
└─────────────┘     └──────────────┘     └───────────────┘     └─────────────┘
Model Context Protocol (MCP) is a capability supported by Anthropic AI models that allow you to create custom tools for any compatible client. MCP clients like Claude Desktop, Cursor, Cline or Zed can run an MCP server which "teaches" these clients about a new tool that they can use.

These tools can call out to external APIs but in our case, all logs are stored locally on your machine and NEVER sent out to any third-party service or API. BrowserTools MCP runs a local instance of a NodeJS API server which communicates with the BrowserTools Chrome Extension.

All consumers of the BrowserTools MCP Server interface with the same NodeJS API and Chrome extension.

Chrome Extension
Monitors XHR requests/responses and console logs
Tracks selected DOM elements
Sends all logs and current element to the BrowserTools Connector
Connects to Websocket server to capture/send screenshots
Allows user to configure token/truncation limits + screenshot folder path
Node Server
Acts as middleware between the Chrome extension and MCP server
Receives logs and currently selected element from Chrome extension
Processes requests from MCP server to capture logs, screenshot or current element
Sends Websocket command to the Chrome extension for capturing a screenshot
Intelligently truncates strings and # of duplicate objects in logs to avoid token limits
Removes cookies and sensitive headers to avoid sending to LLMs in MCP clients
MCP Server
Implements the Model Context Protocol
Provides standardized tools for AI clients
Compatible with various MCP clients (Cursor, Cline, Zed, Claude Desktop, etc.)
Installation
Installation steps can be found in our documentation:

BrowserTools MCP Docs
Usage
Once installed and configured, the system allows any compatible MCP client to:

Monitor browser console output
Capture network traffic
Take screenshots
Analyze selected elements
Wipe logs stored in our MCP server
Run accessibility, performance, SEO, and best practices audits
Compatibility
Works with any MCP-compatible client
Primarily designed for Cursor IDE integration
Supports other AI editors and MCP clients

+ # Rule: Using BrowserTools MCP for Browser Interaction
+ 
+ ## 1. Overview & Purpose
+ 
+ **BrowserTools MCP** is a system that connects an AI agent to a user's web browser. It allows you to see what the user sees, inspect logs, analyze performance, and interact with web page elements. This enables you to debug web applications, suggest UI improvements, and perform comprehensive audits directly from the IDE.
+ 
+ ## 2. Core Architecture
+ 
+ To use this tool, you must understand its three-part structure. All communication happens locally on the user's machine.
+ 
+ 1.  **MCP Client (e.g., Cursor IDE):** This is the environment where you, the AI agent, operate.
+ 2.  **MCP Server (`@agentdeskai/browser-tools-mcp`):** This is the protocol layer installed in the IDE. It provides the standardized tools you can call.
+ 3.  **Node Server (`@agentdeskai/browser-tools-server`):** This is a middleware server running in a separate terminal. It communicates with the Chrome Extension.
+ 4.  **Chrome Extension:** This runs in the user's browser, capturing data and executing commands.
+ 
+ ```mermaid
+ graph TD
+     A[MCP Client / IDE] <--> B[MCP Server: @agentdeskai/browser-tools-mcp];
+     B <--> C[Node Server: @agentdeskai/browser-tools-server];
+     C <--> D[Chrome Extension];
+ ```
+ 
+ **Key Takeaway:** The user must have **both** the Node server running in a terminal **and** the MCP server configured in their IDE for your tools to work.
+ 
+ ## 3. Available Tools & Capabilities
+ 
+ Here are the tools available to you, grouped by category.
+ 
+ ### 3.1. Debugging & Log Analysis
+ 
+ These tools are for inspecting browser activity to diagnose problems.
+ 
+ | Tool Name | Description | When to Use | Example User Prompts |
+ | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
+ | `Get Console Logs` | Returns the most recent console log messages. | When debugging general application behavior. | "Check the console logs." |
+ | `Get Console Errors` | Returns only the most recent console *errors*. | When a user reports something is broken. **This is a primary debugging tool.** | "What went wrong?" "Are there any errors?" |
+ | `Get XHR Network Logs` | Returns recent successful network requests (2xx-3xx status). | To verify that data is being fetched correctly. | "Did the API call succeed?" |
+ | `Get XHR Network Errors` | Returns recent failed network requests (4xx-5xx status). | When data is not loading or saving. **This is a primary debugging tool.**| "Why isn't the data showing up?" |
+ | `Wipe Logs` | Deletes all logs stored on the server. | When the user wants a clean slate for debugging without refreshing the page. | "Wipe the logs." |
+ 
+ ### 3.2. UI Interaction & Editing
+ 
+ These tools allow you to "see" and interact with UI elements.
+ 
+ | Tool Name | Description | When to Use | Example User Prompts |
+ | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
+ | `Capture Screenshot` | Takes a screenshot of the current browser tab and saves it. | When the user describes a visual bug or wants you to see the UI. | "Something looks wrong, can you take a look?" "Take a screenshot." |
+ | `Get currently selected element` | Returns the HTML of the element the user has selected with Chrome DevTools. | When a user wants you to edit, analyze, or find a specific UI component in the codebase. **You must instruct the user to select the element first.** | "Can you change this button?" "Find this element in the code." |
+ 
+ ### 3.3. Auditing & Analysis
+ 
+ These tools use Lighthouse and Puppeteer to run comprehensive audits on the current page.
+ 
+ | Tool Name | Description | When to Use | Example User Prompts |
+ | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
+ | `runAccessibilityAudit` | Checks for WCAG compliance issues. | To ensure the page is usable for people with disabilities. | "Run an accessibility audit." |
+ | `runPerformanceAudit` | Identifies performance bottlenecks. | When a user complains that a page is slow. | "Why is this page loading so slowly?" |
+ | `runSEOAudit` | Evaluates on-page SEO factors. | To improve a page's search engine ranking. | "How can I improve SEO for this page?" |
+ | `runBestPracticesAudit`| Checks for general web development best practices. | For a general code quality and standards review. | "Check for best practices issues." |
+ | `runNextJSAudit` | Runs a specialized audit for Next.JS applications. | When the user confirms they are using Next.JS and wants an audit. | "Run a NextJS audit." |
+ 
+ ### 3.4. Execution Modes
+ 
+ These are high-level tools that run a sequence of other tools.
+ 
+ | Tool Name | Description | When to Use | Example User Prompts |
+ | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
+ | `runDebuggerMode` | Executes all debugging tools (`Get Console Logs`, `Get XHR Logs`, etc.) in sequence. | As a first step for any general debugging request. **This is your most powerful debugging tool.** | "Enter debugger mode." "Debug this page." |
+ | `runAuditMode` | Executes all auditing tools (`runAccessibilityAudit`, `runPerformanceAudit`, etc.) in sequence. | For a complete, high-level analysis of the current page. | "Run audit mode." "Give me a full audit of this page." |
+ 
+ ## 4. Standard Workflows
+ 
+ Follow these workflows for common scenarios.
+ 
+ ### Workflow 1: General Debugging
+ 
+ 1.  **User says:** "Something is broken." or "Why isn't this working?"
+ 2.  **Your action:** Immediately use the `runDebuggerMode` tool.
+ 3.  **Analyze the output:** Review the combined logs to identify the root cause.
+ 4.  **Follow up:** Based on the findings, suggest a fix or ask for more information.
+ 
+ ### Workflow 2: Editing a UI Element
+ 
+ 1.  **User says:** "Change this button to be blue."
+ 2.  **Your action:** Respond with, "Please use the element selector in Chrome DevTools to select the button you want me to change, and then let me know when you have done so."
+ 3.  **User confirms:** "Okay, I've selected it."
+ 4.  **Your action:** Use the `Get currently selected element` tool.
+ 5.  **Analyze the output:** Use the returned HTML to locate the component in the user's codebase and apply the requested changes.
+ 
+ ### Workflow 3: Comprehensive Page Review
+ 
+ 1.  **User says:** "Can you review this page for any issues?"
+ 2.  **Your action:** Use the `runAuditMode` tool.
+ 3.  **Analyze the output:** Synthesize the results from all audits (Performance, SEO, Accessibility, etc.) into a single, prioritized list of recommendations for the user.
+ 
+ ## 5. Important Considerations
+ 
+ - **Screenshots:** By default, screenshots are saved to `~/Downloads/mcp-screenshots`. Advise the user to configure this path to their project workspace for easier access. The "Allow Auto-Paste into Cursor" feature can streamline this.
+ - **Data Privacy:** Reassure the user that all logs and data are processed **locally** and are never sent to any third-party service. The Node server automatically strips sensitive information like cookies and headers.
+ - **Tool Failure:** If a tool fails, it's likely due to a disconnected component. Advise the user to check that a) their Chrome browser is running, b) they have only one DevTools panel open, and c) the `browser-tools-server` is still running in their terminal. 
```

</details>

---

Based on the logs you've provided, here's an explanation of the error affecting your MCP server.

### The Core Problem: Invalid JSON Data

The error message `Client error for command Unexpected token 'R', "Received W"... is not valid JSON` is the key.

Here’s what it means:
1.  **Expected Format:** The MCP server is designed to receive information formatted as **JSON**. A valid JSON object must start with a `{` character (or `[` for an array).
2.  **Actual Data Received:** Instead of JSON, the server is receiving a plain text string that starts with the letter "R" (from a message like "**R**eceived **W**arning...") or "U" (from "**U**nhandled...").
3.  **The Crash:** The server's JSON parser tries to read the string, sees the first letter ('R' or 'U'), and immediately fails because it's not the `{` it was expecting. This triggers the "Unexpected token" error.

In short, one of the components in your system is sending a plain-text error or warning message to an endpoint that only accepts structured JSON data.

### Likely Causes

This issue typically happens in a multi-component system like the one you've built (`Client` -> `MCP Server` -> `Node Server` -> `Extension`).

1.  **Unhandled Promise Rejection (Most Likely):** The log message `Unhandled ... is not valid JSON` strongly suggests that a JavaScript/Node.js process (like your `context7_server` or `sequential-thinking` server) encountered an error that wasn't properly handled (an "unhandled promise rejection"). The Node.js process then sends this plain-text error message back to the MCP server, which can't parse it.

2.  **Error Bubbling:** A downstream service (like the RAG server or a web search tool) fails and returns a simple string error (e.g., "Received Warning: Connection timed out"). Your `agent_orchestrator` then passes this raw string along to another agent or back to the client, causing the JSON parsing error there.

3.  **Misconfigured Client/Agent:** It's possible an agent is configured to send a plain string in a situation where it should be wrapping its response in a JSON object. Your `advanced_router_web_chat/main.py` has several fallback mechanisms for `JSONDecodeError`, which indicates this is a known failure point in the system.

### How to Fix It

The solution is to find which component is sending the plain text and ensure it wraps **all** of its responses—especially errors—in a valid JSON structure.

Instead of sending: `Unhandled Rejection: Cannot connect to service`

It should send something like:
```json
{
  "status": "error",
  "message": "Unhandled Rejection: Cannot connect to service"
}
```

This way, the receiving server can correctly parse the JSON and handle the error gracefully instead of crashing. You should investigate the logs of your Node.js-based MCP servers first, as they are the most likely source of the "Unhandled" rejection messages.

_**User (2025-06-05 10:26Z)**_

Remove the console.log line from the main() function.
The reason is that the MCP protocol expects JSON messages, but we're writing plain text to stdout.

Replace console.log with process.stderr.write('...'); wherever required.

on Apr 23
Remove the console.log line from the main() function. The reason is that the MCP protocol expects JSON messages, but we're writing plain text to stdout.

Replace console.log with process.stderr.write('...'); wherever required.

This appears to be a wide-spread issue with MCP servers using the stdio transport: https://github.com/mlshv/mcps-logger

I'm now in the process of tracking down the cached package location. The initial step involves executing `npm config get cache` to determine the cache directory. I anticipate this will lead me to a path where `@agentdeskai/browser-tools-server` resides. ---

Of course. This is a classic problem with `stdio` MCP servers. The fix you've identified is correct: informational logs need to be written to `stderr` so they don't corrupt the JSON data being passed over `stdout`.

I can guide you through finding the file, editing it, and deploying the fix. We will essentially be patching the file that `npx` downloads and runs.

First, I need to locate where `npx` stores the cached packages. I'll start by listing the contents of a common `npx` cache directory to find the `browser-tools-server` package.

+     async handleScreenshot(req, res) {
+         if (!this.activeConnection) {
+             return res.status(503).json({ error: "Chrome extension not connected" });
+         }
+         try {
+             const result = await new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
+                 // Set up one-time message handler for this screenshot request
+                 const messageHandler = (message) => {
+                     try {
+                         const response = JSON.parse(message.toString());
+                         if (response.type === "screenshot-error") {
+                             reject(new Error(response.error));
+                             return;
+                         }
+                         if (response.type === "screenshot-data" &&
+                             response.data &&
+                             response.path) {
+                             // Remove the data:image/png;base64, prefix
+                             const base64Data = response.data.replace(/^data:image\/png;base64,/, "");
+                             // Ensure the directory exists
+                             const dir = path.dirname(response.path);
+                             fs.mkdirSync(dir, { recursive: true });
+                             // Generate a unique filename using timestamp

+                             const filename = `screenshot-${timestamp}.png`;

+                             fs.writeFileSync(fullPath, base64Data, "base64");
+                             resolve({
+                                 path: fullPath,
+                                 filename: filename,
+                             });
+                         }
+                     }
+                     catch (error) {
+                         reject(error);
+                     }
+                     finally {
+                         this.activeConnection?.removeListener("message", messageHandler);
+                     }
+                 };
+                 // Add temporary message handler
+                 this.activeConnection?.on("message", messageHandler);
+                 // Request screenshot
+                 this.activeConnection?.send(JSON.stringify({ type: "take-screenshot" }));

+                     this.activeConnection?.removeListener("message", messageHandler);

+                     process.stderr.write(`URL became available after waiting: ${currentUrl}\n`);

+                 process.stderr.write(`Browser Connector: Setting up screenshot callback for requestId: ${requestId}\n`);
+                 // Store callback in map
+                 screenshotCallbacks.set(requestId, { resolve, reject });
+                 process.stderr.write("Browser Connector: Current callbacks: " + Array.from(screenshotCallbacks.keys()) + "\n");
+                 // Set timeout to clean up if we don't get a response
+                 setTimeout(() => {
+                     if (screenshotCallbacks.has(requestId)) {
+                         process.stderr.write(`Browser Connector: Screenshot capture timed out for requestId: ${requestId}\n`);
+                         screenshotCallbacks.delete(requestId);
+                         reject(new Error("Screenshot capture timed out - no response from Chrome extension"));
+                     }
+                 }, 10000);
+             });
+             // Send screenshot request to extension
+             const message = JSON.stringify({
+                 type: "take-screenshot",
+                 requestId: requestId,
+             });
+             process.stderr.write(`Browser Connector: Sending WebSocket message to extension: ` + message + "\n");
+             this.activeConnection.send(message);
+             // Wait for screenshot data
+             process.stderr.write("Browser Connector: Waiting for screenshot data...\n");
+             const { data: base64Data, path: customPath, autoPaste, } = await screenshotPromise;
+             process.stderr.write("Browser Connector: Received screenshot data, saving...\n");
+             process.stderr.write("Browser Connector: Custom path from extension: " + customPath + "\n");
+             process.stderr.write("Browser Connector: Auto-paste setting: " + autoPaste + "\n");
+             // Always prioritize the path from the Chrome extension
+             let targetPath = customPath;
+             // If no path provided by extension, fall back to defaults
+             if (!targetPath) {
+                 targetPath =
+                     currentSettings.screenshotPath || getDefaultDownloadsFolder();
+             }
+             // Convert the path for the current platform
+             targetPath = convertPathForCurrentPlatform(targetPath);
+             process.stderr.write(`Browser Connector: Using path: ${targetPath}\n`);
+             if (!base64Data) {
+                 throw new Error("No screenshot data received from Chrome extension");
+             }
+             try {
+                 fs.mkdirSync(targetPath, { recursive: true });
+                 process.stderr.write(`Browser Connector: Created directory: ${targetPath}\n`);
+             }
+             catch (err) {
+                 console.error(`Browser Connector: Error creating directory: ${targetPath}`, err);
+                 throw new Error(`Failed to create screenshot directory: ${err instanceof Error ? err.message : String(err)}`);
+             }
+             const timestamp = new Date().toISOString().replace(/[:.]/g, "-");
+             const filename = `screenshot-${timestamp}.png`;
+             const fullPath = path.join(targetPath, filename);
+             process.stderr.write(`Browser Connector: Full screenshot path: ${fullPath}\n`);
+             // Remove the data:image/png;base64, prefix if present
+             const cleanBase64 = base64Data.replace(/^data:image\/png;base64,/, "");
+             // Save the file
+             try {
+                 fs.writeFileSync(fullPath, cleanBase64, "base64");
+                 process.stderr.write(`Browser Connector: Screenshot saved to: ${fullPath}\n`);
+             }
+             catch (err) {
+                 console.error(`Browser Connector: Error saving screenshot to: ${fullPath}`, err);
+                 throw new Error(`Failed to save screenshot: ${err instanceof Error ? err.message : String(err)}`);
+             }
+             // Check if running on macOS before executing AppleScript
+             if (os.platform() === "darwin" && autoPaste === true) {
+                 process.stderr.write("Browser Connector: Running on macOS with auto-paste enabled, executing AppleScript to paste into Cursor\n");
+                 // Create the AppleScript to copy the image to clipboard and paste into Cursor
+                 // This version is more robust and includes debugging
+                 const appleScript = `
+           -- Set path to the screenshot
+           set imagePath to "${fullPath}"
+           
+           -- Copy the image to clipboard
+           try
+             set the clipboard to (read (POSIX file imagePath) as «class PNGf»)
+           on error errMsg
+             log "Error copying image to clipboard: " & errMsg
+             return "Failed to copy image to clipboard: " & errMsg
+           end try
+           
+           -- Activate Cursor application
+           try
+             tell application "Cursor"
+               activate
+             end tell
+           on error errMsg
+             log "Error activating Cursor: " & errMsg
+             return "Failed to activate Cursor: " & errMsg
+           end try
+           
+           -- Wait for the application to fully activate
+           delay 3
+           
+           -- Try to interact with Cursor
+           try
+             tell application "System Events"
+               tell process "Cursor"
+                 -- Get the frontmost window
+                 if (count of windows) is 0 then
+                   return "No windows found in Cursor"
+                 end if
+                 
+                 set cursorWindow to window 1
+                 
+                 -- Try Method 1: Look for elements of class "Text Area"
+                 set foundElements to {}
+                 
+                 -- Try different selectors to find the text input area
+                 try
+                   -- Try with class
+                   set textAreas to UI elements of cursorWindow whose class is "Text Area"
+                   if (count of textAreas) > 0 then
+                     set foundElements to textAreas
+                   end if
+                 end try
+                 
+                 if (count of foundElements) is 0 then
+                   try
+                     -- Try with AXTextField role
+                     set textFields to UI elements of cursorWindow whose role is "AXTextField"
+                     if (count of textFields) > 0 then
+                       set foundElements to textFields
+                     end if
+                   end try
+                 end if
+                 
+                 if (count of foundElements) is 0 then
+                   try
+                     -- Try with AXTextArea role in nested elements
+                     set allElements to UI elements of cursorWindow
+                     repeat with anElement in allElements
+                       try
+                         set childElements to UI elements of anElement
+                         repeat with aChild in childElements
+                           try
+                             if role of aChild is "AXTextArea" or role of aChild is "AXTextField" then
+                               set end of foundElements to aChild
+                             end if
+                           end try
+                         end repeat
+                       end try
+                     end repeat
+                   end try
+                 end if
+                 
+                 -- If no elements found with specific attributes, try a broader approach
+                 if (count of foundElements) is 0 then
+                   -- Just try to use the Command+V shortcut on the active window
+                    -- This assumes Cursor already has focus on the right element
+                     keystroke "v" using command down
+                     delay 1
+                     keystroke "here is the screenshot"
+                     delay 1
+                    -- Try multiple methods to press Enter
+                    key code 36 -- Use key code for Return key
+                    delay 0.5
+                    keystroke return -- Use keystroke return as alternative
+                     return "Used fallback method: Command+V on active window"
+                 else
+                   -- We found a potential text input element
+                   set inputElement to item 1 of foundElements
+                   
+                   -- Try to focus and paste
+                   try
+                     set focused of inputElement to true
+                     delay 0.5
+                     
+                     -- Paste the image
+                     keystroke "v" using command down
+                     delay 1
+                     
+                     -- Type the text
+                     keystroke "here is the screenshot"
+                     delay 1
+                     -- Try multiple methods to press Enter
+                     key code 36 -- Use key code for Return key
+                     delay 0.5
+                     keystroke return -- Use keystroke return as alternative
+                     return "Successfully pasted screenshot into Cursor text element"
+                   on error errMsg
+                     log "Error interacting with found element: " & errMsg
+                     -- Fallback to just sending the key commands
+                     keystroke "v" using command down
+                     delay 1
+                     keystroke "here is the screenshot"
+                     delay 1
+                     -- Try multiple methods to press Enter
+                     key code 36 -- Use key code for Return key
+                     delay 0.5
+                     keystroke return -- Use keystroke return as alternative
+                     return "Used fallback after element focus error: " & errMsg
+                   end try
+                 end if
+               end tell
+             end tell
+           on error errMsg
+             log "Error in System Events block: " & errMsg
+             return "Failed in System Events: " & errMsg
+           end try
+         `;
+                 // Execute the AppleScript
+                 exec(`osascript -e '${appleScript}'`, (error, stdout, stderr) => {
+                     if (error) {

+                     else {
+                         process.stderr.write(`Browser Connector: AppleScript executed successfully\n`);

+                     }

+             }

+     // Add shutdown method
+     shutdown() {
+         return new Promise((resolve) => {
+             process.stderr.write("Shutting down WebSocket server...\n");
+             // Send close message to client if connection is active
+             if (this.activeConnection &&
+                 this.activeConnection.readyState === WebSocket.OPEN) {

+             this.wss.close(() => {
+                 clearTimeout(forceCloseTimeout);
+                 process.stderr.write("WebSocket server closed gracefully\n");

+             });

+     }
+     // Sets up the accessibility audit endpoint
+     setupAccessibilityAudit() {
+         this.setupAuditEndpoint(AuditCategory.ACCESSIBILITY, "/accessibility-audit", runAccessibilityAudit);
+     }
+     // Sets up the performance audit endpoint
+     setupPerformanceAudit() {
+         this.setupAuditEndpoint(AuditCategory.PERFORMANCE, "/performance-audit", runPerformanceAudit);
+     }
+     // Set up SEO audit endpoint
+     setupSEOAudit() {
+         this.setupAuditEndpoint(AuditCategory.SEO, "/seo-audit", runSEOAudit);
+     }
+     // Add a setup method for Best Practices audit
+     setupBestPracticesAudit() {
+         this.setupAuditEndpoint(AuditCategory.BEST_PRACTICES, "/best-practices-audit", runBestPracticesAudit);
+     }
+     /**
+      * Generic method to set up an audit endpoint
+      * @param auditType The type of audit (accessibility, performance, SEO)
+      * @param endpoint The endpoint path
+      * @param auditFunction The audit function to call
+      */
+     setupAuditEndpoint(auditType, endpoint, auditFunction) {
+         // Add server identity validation endpoint
+         this.app.get("/.identity", (req, res) => {
+             res.json({
+                 signature: "mcp-browser-connector-24x7",
+                 version: "1.2.0",
+             });
+         });
+         this.app.post(endpoint, async (req, res) => {
+             try {
+                 process.stderr.write(`${auditType} audit request received\n`);
+                 // Get URL using our helper method
+                 const url = await this.getUrlForAudit();
+                 if (!url) {
+                     process.stderr.write(`No URL available for ${auditType} audit\n`);
+                     return res.status(400).json({
+                         error: `URL is required for ${auditType} audit. Make sure you navigate to a page in the browser first, and the browser-tool extension tab is open.`,
+                     });
+                 }
+                 // If we're using the stored URL (not from request body), log it now
+                 if (!req.body?.url && url === currentUrl) {
+                     process.stderr.write(`Using stored URL for ${auditType} audit: ` + url + "\n");
+                 }
+                 // Check if we're using the default URL
+                 if (url === "about:blank") {
+                     process.stderr.write(`Cannot run ${auditType} audit on about:blank\n`);
+                     return res.status(400).json({
+                         error: `Cannot run ${auditType} audit on about:blank`,
+                     });
+                 }
+                 process.stderr.write(`Preparing to run ${auditType} audit for: ${url}\n`);
+                 // Run the audit using the provided function
+                 try {
+                     const result = await auditFunction(url);
+                     process.stderr.write(`${auditType} audit completed successfully\n`);
+                     // Return the results
+                     res.json(result);
+                 }
+                 catch (auditError) {
+                     console.error(`${auditType} audit failed:`, auditError);
+                     const errorMessage = auditError instanceof Error
+                         ? auditError.message
+                         : String(auditError);
+                     res.status(500).json({
+                         error: `Failed to run ${auditType} audit: ${errorMessage}`,
+                     });
+                 }
+             }
+             catch (error) {
+                 console.error(`Error in ${auditType} audit endpoint:`, error);
+                 const errorMessage = error instanceof Error ? error.message : String(error);
+                 res.status(500).json({
+                     error: `Error in ${auditType} audit endpoint: ${errorMessage}`,
+                 });
+             }
+         });
+     }
+ }
+ // Use an async IIFE to allow for async/await in the initial setup
+ (async () => {
+     try {
+         process.stderr.write(`Starting Browser Tools Server...\n`);
+         process.stderr.write(`Requested port: ${REQUESTED_PORT}\n`);
+         // Find an available port
+         try {
+             PORT = await getAvailablePort(REQUESTED_PORT);
+             if (PORT !== REQUESTED_PORT) {
+                 process.stderr.write(`\n====================================\n`);
+                 process.stderr.write(`NOTICE: Requested port ${REQUESTED_PORT} was in use.\n`);
+                 process.stderr.write(`Using port ${PORT} instead.\n`);
+                 process.stderr.write(`====================================\n\n`);

+         }

+             process.stderr.write(`\n=== Browser Tools Server Started ===\n`);

+             if (PORT !== REQUESTED_PORT) {
+                 process.stderr.write(`NOTE: Using fallback port ${PORT} instead of requested port ${REQUESTED_PORT}\n`);
+             }
+             // Log all available network interfaces for easier discovery
+             const networkInterfaces = os.networkInterfaces();

                                                                                             \|                     \|                  \|<br/><br/>## Repository files navigation<br/><br/>* README<br/>* MIT license<br/><br/># BrowserTools MCP<br/><br/>> Make your AI tools 10x more aware and capable of interacting with your browser<br/><br/>This application is a powerful browser monitoring and interaction tool that enables AI-powered applications via Anthropic's Model Context Protocol (MCP) to capture and analyze browser data through a Chrome extension.<br/><br/>Read our docs for the full installation, quickstart and contribution guides.<br/><br/>## Roadmap<br/><br/>Check out our project roadmap here: Github Roadmap / Project Board<br/><br/>## Updates<br/><br/>v1.2.0 is out! Here's a quick breakdown of the update:<br/><br/>* You can now enable "Allow Auto-Paste into Cursor" within the DevTools panel. Screenshots will be automatically pasted into Cursor (just make sure to focus/click into the Agent input field in Cursor, otherwise it won't work!)<br/>* Integrated a suite of SEO, performance, accessibility, and best practice analysis tools via Lighthouse<br/>* Implemented a NextJS specific prompt used to improve SEO for a NextJS application<br/>* Added Debugger Mode as a tool which executes all debugging tools in a particular sequence, along with a prompt to improve reasoning<br/>* Added Audit Mode as a tool to execute all auditing tools in a particular sequence<br/>* Resolved Windows connectivity issues<br/>* Improved networking between BrowserTools server, extension and MCP server with host/port auto-discovery, auto-reconnect, and graceful shutdown mechanisms<br/>* Added ability to more easily exit out of the Browser Tools server with Ctrl+C<br/><br/>## Quickstart Guide<br/><br/>There are three components to run this MCP tool:<br/><br/>1. Install our chrome extension from here: v1.2.0 BrowserToolsMCP Chrome Extension<br/>2. Install the MCP server from this command within your IDE: `npx @agentdeskai/browser-tools-mcp@latest`<br/>3. Open a new terminal and run this command: `npx @agentdeskai/browser-tools-server@latest`<br/>* Different IDEs have different configs but this command is generally a good starting point; please reference your IDEs docs for the proper config setup<br/><br/>IMPORTANT TIP - there are two servers you need to install. There's...<br/><br/>* browser-tools-server (local nodejs server that's a middleware for gathering logs) and<br/>* browser-tools-mcp (MCP server that you install into your IDE that communicates w/ the extension + browser-tools-server)<br/><br/>`npx @agentdeskai/browser-tools-mcp@latest` is what you put into your IDE`npx @agentdeskai/browser-tools-server@latest` is what you run in a new terminal window<br/><br/>After those three steps, open up your chrome dev tools and then the BrowserToolsMCP panel.<br/><br/>If you're still having issues try these steps:<br/><br/>* Quit / close down your browser. Not just the window but all of Chrome itself.<br/>* Restart the local node server (browser-tools-server)<br/>* Make sure you only have ONE instance of chrome dev tools panel open<br/><br/>After that, it should work but if it doesn't let me know and I can share some more steps to gather logs/info about the issue!<br/><br/>If you have any questions or issues, feel free to open an issue ticket! And if you have any ideas to make this better, feel free to reach out or open an issue ticket with an enhancement tag or reach out to me at @tedx\_ai on x<br/><br/>## Full Update Notes:<br/><br/>Coding agents like Cursor can run these audits against the current page seamlessly. By leveraging Puppeteer and the Lighthouse npm library, BrowserTools MCP can now:<br/><br/>* Evaluate pages for WCAG compliance<br/>* Identify performance bottlenecks<br/>* Flag on-page SEO issues<br/>* Check adherence to web development best practices<br/>* Review NextJS specific issues with SEO<br/><br/>...all without leaving your IDE 🎉<br/><br/>---<br/><br/>## 🔑 Key Additions<br/><br/>\| Audit Type         \| Description                                                                                                                              \|<br/>\| ------------------ \| ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- \|<br/>\| **Accessibility**  \| WCAG-compliant checks for color contrast, missing alt text, keyboard navigation traps, ARIA attributes, and more.                        \|<br/>\| **Performance**    \| Lighthouse-driven analysis of render-blocking resources, excessive DOM size, unoptimized images, and other factors affecting page speed. \|<br/>\| **SEO**            \| Evaluates on-page SEO factors (like metadata, headings, and link structure) and suggests improvements for better search visibility.      \|<br/>\| **Best Practices** \| Checks for general best practices in web development.                                                                                    \|<br/>\| **NextJS Audit**   \| Injects a prompt used to perform a NextJS audit.                                                                                         \|<br/>\| **Audit Mode**     \| Runs all auditing tools in a sequence.                                                                                                   \|<br/>\| **Debugger Mode**  \| Runs all debugging tools in a sequence.                                                                                                  \|<br/><br/>---<br/><br/>## 🛠️ Using Audit Tools<br/><br/>### ✅ **Before You Start**<br/><br/>Ensure you have:<br/><br/>* An **active tab** in your browser<br/>* The **BrowserTools extension enabled**<br/><br/>### ▶️ **Running Audits**<br/><br/>**Headless Browser Automation**:  <br/>Puppeteer automates a headless Chrome instance to load the page and collect audit data, ensuring accurate results even for SPAs or content loaded via JavaScript.<br/><br/>The headless browser instance remains active for **60 seconds** after the last audit call to efficiently handle consecutive audit requests.<br/><br/>**Structured Results**:  <br/>Each audit returns results in a structured JSON format, including overall scores and detailed issue lists. This makes it easy for MCP-compatible clients to interpret the findings and present actionable insights.<br/><br/>The MCP server provides tools to run audits on the current page. Here are example queries you can use to trigger them:<br/><br/>#### Accessibility Audit (`runAccessibilityAudit`)<br/><br/>Ensures the page meets accessibility standards like WCAG.<br/><br/>> **Example Queries:**<br/>> <br/>> * "Are there any accessibility issues on this page?"<br/>> * "Run an accessibility audit."<br/>> * "Check if this page meets WCAG standards."<br/><br/>#### Performance Audit (`runPerformanceAudit`)<br/><br/>Identifies performance bottlenecks and loading issues.<br/><br/>> **Example Queries:**<br/>> <br/>> * "Why is this page loading so slowly?"<br/>> * "Check the performance of this page."<br/>> * "Run a performance audit."<br/><br/>#### SEO Audit (`runSEOAudit`)<br/><br/>Evaluates how well the page is optimized for search engines.<br/><br/>> **Example Queries:**<br/>> <br/>> * "How can I improve SEO for this page?"<br/>> * "Run an SEO audit."<br/>> * "Check SEO on this page."<br/><br/>#### Best Practices Audit (`runBestPracticesAudit`)<br/><br/>Checks for general best practices in web development.<br/><br/>> **Example Queries:**<br/>> <br/>> * "Run a best practices audit."<br/>> * "Check best practices on this page."<br/>> * "Are there any best practices issues on this page?"<br/><br/>#### Audit Mode (`runAuditMode`)<br/><br/>Runs all audits in a particular sequence. Will run a NextJS audit if the framework is detected.<br/><br/>> **Example Queries:**<br/>> <br/>> * "Run audit mode."<br/>> * "Enter audit mode."<br/><br/>#### NextJS Audits (`runNextJSAudit`)<br/><br/>Checks for best practices and SEO improvements for NextJS applications<br/><br/>> **Example Queries:**<br/>> <br/>> * "Run a NextJS audit."<br/>> * "Run a NextJS audit, I'm using app router."<br/>> * "Run a NextJS audit, I'm using page router."<br/><br/>#### Debugger Mode (`runDebuggerMode`)<br/><br/>Runs all debugging tools in a particular sequence<br/><br/>> **Example Queries:**<br/>> <br/>> * "Enter debugger mode."<br/><br/>## Architecture<br/><br/>There are three core components all used to capture and analyze browser data:<br/><br/>1. **Chrome Extension**: A browser extension that captures screenshots, console logs, network activity and DOM elements.<br/>2. **Node Server**: An intermediary server that facilitates communication between the Chrome extension and any instance of an MCP server.<br/>3. **MCP Server**: A Model Context Protocol server that provides standardized tools for AI clients to interact with the browser.<br/><br/>```<br/>┌─────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐     ┌───────────────┐     ┌─────────────┐<br/>│  MCP Client │ ──► │  MCP Server  │ ──► │  Node Server  │ ──► │   Chrome    │<br/>│  (e.g.      │ ◄── │  (Protocol   │ ◄── │ (Middleware)  │ ◄── │  Extension  │<br/>│   Cursor)   │     │   Handler)   │     │               │     │             │<br/>└─────────────┘     └──────────────┘     └───────────────┘     └─────────────┘<br/><br/>```<br/><br/>Model Context Protocol (MCP) is a capability supported by Anthropic AI models that allow you to create custom tools for any compatible client. MCP clients like Claude Desktop, Cursor, Cline or Zed can run an MCP server which "teaches" these clients about a new tool that they can use.<br/><br/>These tools can call out to external APIs but in our case, **all logs are stored locally** on your machine and NEVER sent out to any third-party service or API. BrowserTools MCP runs a local instance of a NodeJS API server which communicates with the BrowserTools Chrome Extension.<br/><br/>All consumers of the BrowserTools MCP Server interface with the same NodeJS API and Chrome extension.<br/><br/>#### Chrome Extension<br/><br/>* Monitors XHR requests/responses and console logs<br/>* Tracks selected DOM elements<br/>* Sends all logs and current element to the BrowserTools Connector<br/>* Connects to Websocket server to capture/send screenshots<br/>* Allows user to configure token/truncation limits + screenshot folder path<br/><br/>#### Node Server<br/><br/>* Acts as middleware between the Chrome extension and MCP server<br/>* Receives logs and currently selected element from Chrome extension<br/>* Processes requests from MCP server to capture logs, screenshot or current element<br/>* Sends Websocket command to the Chrome extension for capturing a screenshot<br/>* Intelligently truncates strings and # of duplicate objects in logs to avoid token limits<br/>* Removes cookies and sensitive headers to avoid sending to LLMs in MCP clients<br/><br/>#### MCP Server<br/><br/>* Implements the Model Context Protocol<br/>* Provides standardized tools for AI clients<br/>* Compatible with various MCP clients (Cursor, Cline, Zed, Claude Desktop, etc.)<br/><br/>## Installation<br/><br/>Installation steps can be found in our documentation:<br/><br/>* BrowserTools MCP Docs<br/><br/>## Usage<br/><br/>Once installed and configured, the system allows any compatible MCP client to:<br/><br/>* Monitor browser console output<br/>* Capture network traffic<br/>* Take screenshots<br/>* Analyze selected elements<br/>* Wipe logs stored in our MCP server<br/>* Run accessibility, performance, SEO, and best practices audits<br/><br/>## Compatibility<br/><br/>* Works with any MCP-compatible client<br/>* Primarily designed for Cursor IDE integration<br/>* Supports other AI editors and MCP clients<br/><br/>## About<br/><br/> Monitor browser logs directly from Cursor and other MCP compatible IDEs.<br/><br/>agentdesk.ai/ <br/><br/>### Topics<br/><br/> ai  mcp  cursor  anthropic  mcp-server <br/><br/>### Resources<br/><br/> Readme <br/><br/>### License<br/><br/> MIT license <br/><br/>###  Uh oh!<br/><br/>There was an error while loading. | Browser Tools MCP server for AI agents | https://playbooks.com/mcp/agentdeskai-browser-tools | playbooks <br/><br/>Learn <br/><br/>Rules<br/><br/> Cursor Rules  Windsurf Rules MCPs Modes Prompts <br/><br/> Log in <br/><br/>Menu  Learn  Rules  Cursor Rules  Windsurf Rules  MCPs  Modes <br/><br/> AI Image Editor <br/><br/> Log in <br/><br/># Browser Tools MCP server<br/><br/> Provides a bridge between web browsers and assistants through a Chrome extension, enabling real-time access to console logs, network requests, screenshots, DOM elements, and Lighthouse audits for debugging and improving web applications.<br/><br/> Back to servers <br/><br/> Provider<br/><br/> AgentDesk AI <br/><br/> Release date<br/><br/> Mar 20, 2025<br/><br/> Language<br/><br/> TypeScript<br/><br/> View on GitHub <br/><br/>This Model Context Protocol (MCP) server helps AI tools analyze and interact with your browser by capturing console logs, network requests, screenshots, and page elements. It creates a bridge between your browser activities and AI-powered applications.<br/><br/>## Installation<br/><br/>### Prerequisites<br/><br/>* Chrome browser<br/>* Node.js environment<br/><br/>### Setup Process<br/><br/>1. Install the MCP server package:  <br/>```bash  <br/>npx @agentdeskai/[email protected]  <br/>```<br/>2. Download the latest Chrome extension:  <br/>   * Get the v1.2.0 BrowserToolsMCP Chrome Extension  <br/>   * Unzip and load it in Chrome through developer mode<br/>3. Run the local node server:  <br/>```bash  <br/>npx @agentdeskai/[email protected]  <br/>```  <br/>> **Note**: Always specify the version (1.2.0) when using npx to ensure you get the latest release.<br/>4. Open Chrome DevTools to start sending logs to your server<br/><br/>## Usage<br/><br/>### Basic Features<br/><br/>The MCP server enables AI tools to:<br/><br/>* Monitor browser console output<br/>* Capture network traffic<br/>* Take screenshots<br/>* Analyze selected DOM elements<br/>* Wipe logs stored in the MCP server<br/><br/>### Screenshot Auto-Paste Feature<br/><br/>Enable "Allow Auto-Paste into Cursor" within the DevTools panel to automatically paste screenshots into Cursor. Make sure to focus on the Agent input field in Cursor for this to work.<br/><br/>### Audit Tools<br/><br/>The MCP server provides several audit tools that can be triggered with specific queries:<br/><br/>#### Accessibility Audit<br/><br/>Checks page compliance with WCAG standards:<br/><br/>> "Are there any accessibility issues on this page?"  <br/>> "Run an accessibility audit."<br/><br/>#### Performance Audit<br/><br/>Identifies loading issues and performance bottlenecks:<br/><br/>> "Why is this page loading slowly?"  <br/>> "Check the performance of this page."<br/><br/>#### SEO Audit<br/><br/>Evaluates search engine optimization factors:<br/><br/>> "How can I improve SEO for this page?"  <br/>> "Run an SEO audit."<br/><br/>#### Best Practices Audit<br/><br/>Checks adherence to web development standards:<br/><br/>> "Run a best practices audit."  <br/>> "Are there any best practices issues on this page?"<br/><br/>#### NextJS Audits<br/><br/>Provides NextJS-specific analysis:<br/><br/>> "Run a NextJS audit."  <br/>> "Run a NextJS audit, I'm using app router."<br/><br/>### Special Modes<br/><br/>#### Audit Mode<br/><br/>Runs all audit tools in sequence:<br/><br/>> "Run audit mode."  <br/>> "Enter audit mode."<br/><br/>#### Debugger Mode<br/><br/>Executes all debugging tools in sequence:<br/><br/>> "Enter debugger mode."<br/><br/>## Architecture<br/><br/>The system consists of three main components:<br/><br/>1. **Chrome Extension**: Captures browser data (screenshots, logs, network activity)<br/>2. **Node Server**: Middleware that processes data and communicates between components<br/>3. **MCP Server**: Implements the Model Context Protocol for AI client interaction<br/><br/>```<br/>┌─────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐     ┌───────────────┐     ┌─────────────┐<br/>│  MCP Client │ ──► │  MCP Server  │ ──► │  Node Server  │ ──► │   Chrome    │<br/>│  (e.g.      │ ◄── │  (Protocol   │ ◄── │ (Middleware)  │ ◄── │  Extension  │<br/>│   Cursor)   │     │   Handler)   │     │               │     │             │<br/>└─────────────┘     └──────────────┘     └───────────────┘     └─────────────┘<br/><br/>```<br/><br/>All logs are stored locally on your machine and are never sent to any third-party service.<br/><br/>## Compatibility<br/><br/>* Works with any MCP-compatible client<br/>* Primary integration with Cursor IDE<br/>* Supports other AI editors and MCP clients<br/><br/>## How to add this MCP server to Cursor<br/><br/>There are two ways to add an MCP server to Cursor. The most common way is to add the server globally in the `~/.cursor/mcp.json` file so that it is available in all of your projects.<br/><br/>If you only need the server in a single project, you can add it to the project instead by creating or adding it to the `.cursor/mcp.json` file.<br/><br/>### Adding an MCP server to Cursor globally<br/><br/>To add a global MCP server go to Cursor Settings > MCP and click "Add new global MCP server".<br/><br/>When you click that button the `~/.cursor/mcp.json` file will be opened and you can add your server like this:<br/><br/>```json<br/>\{<br/>    "mcpServers": \{<br/>        "cursor-rules-mcp": \{<br/>            "command": "npx",<br/>            "args": [<br/>                "-y",<br/>                "cursor-rules-mcp"<br/>            ]<br/>        \}<br/>    \}<br/>\}<br/><br/>```<br/><br/>### Adding an MCP server to a project<br/><br/>To add an MCP server to a project you can create a new `.cursor/mcp.json` file or add it to the existing one. This will look exactly the same as the global MCP server example above.<br/><br/>### How to use the MCP server<br/><br/>Once the server is installed, you might need to head back to Settings > MCP and click the refresh button.<br/><br/>The Cursor agent will then be able to see the available tools the added MCP server has available and will call them when it needs to.<br/><br/>You can also explictly ask the agent to use the tool by mentioning the tool name and describing what the function does.<br/><br/>Privacy / Terms <br/><br/> by Ian Nuttall <br/><br/>Want to 10x your AI skills?<br/><br/> Get a free account and learn to code + market your apps using AI (with or without vibes!).<br/><br/> Continue with Google  Continue with GitHub <br/><br/>Nah, maybe later |

             \|                                                                                                                  \|                     \|                  \|<br/><br/>## Repository files navigation<br/><br/>* README<br/>* Code of conduct<br/>* MIT license<br/><br/>  <br/># agentd<br/><br/> A daemon that makes a desktop OS accessible to AI agents.  <br/>**Explore the docs »**   <br/>  <br/>View Demo ·Report Bug ·Request Feature <br/><br/>  <br/>`AgentD` makes a desktop OS accessible to AI agents by exposing an HTTP API.<br/><br/>For a higher level interface see AgentDesk.<br/><br/>## Usage<br/><br/>`AgentD` is currently tested on Ubuntu 22.04 cloud image.<br/><br/>We recommend using one of our base vms which is already configured.<br/><br/>### Qemu<br/><br/>For Qemu, download the qcow2 image:<br/><br/>wget https://storage.googleapis.com/agentsea-vms/jammy/latest/agentd-jammy.qcow2<br/><br/>To use the image, we need to make a cloud-init iso with our user-data. See this tutorial, below is how it looks on MacOS:<br/><br/>xorriso -as mkisofs -o cidata.iso -V "cidata" -J -r -iso-level 3 meta/<br/><br/>Then the image can be ran with Qemu:<br/><br/>qemu-system-x86_64 -nographic -hda ./agentd-jammy.qcow2 \<br/>-m 4G -smp 2 -netdev user,id=vmnet,hostfwd=tcp::6080-:6080,hostfwd=tcp::8000-:8000,hostfwd=tcp::2222-:22 \<br/>-device e1000,netdev=vmnet -cdrom cidata.iso<br/><br/>Once running, the agentd service can be accessed:<br/><br/>curl localhost:8000/health<br/><br/>To login to the machine:<br/><br/>ssh -p 2222 agentsea@localhost<br/><br/>### AWS<br/><br/>For AWS, use public AMI `ami-01a893c1530453073`.<br/><br/>Create a cloud-init script with your ssh key:<br/><br/>#cloud-config<br/><br/>users:<br/>  - name: agentsea<br/>    sudo: ['ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD:ALL']<br/>    groups: sudo<br/>    ssh_authorized_keys:<br/>      - your-ssh-public-key<br/><br/>package_upgrade: true<br/><br/>aws ec2 run-instances \<br/>    --image-id ami-01a893c1530453073 \<br/>    --count 1 \<br/>    --instance-type t2.micro \<br/>    --key-name $KEY_NAME \<br/>    --security-group-ids $SG_NAME \<br/>    --subnet-id $SUBNET_NAME \<br/>    --user-data file://path/to/cloud-init-config.yaml<br/><br/>### GCE<br/><br/>For GCE, use the public image `ubuntu-22-04-20240208044623`.<br/><br/>gcloud compute instances create $NAME \<br/>    --machine-type "n1-standard-1" \<br/>    --image "ubuntu-22-04-20240208044623" \<br/>    --image-project $PROJECT_ID \<br/>    --zone $ZONE \<br/>    --metadata ssh-keys="agentsea:$(cat path/to/your/public/ssh/key.pub)"<br/><br/>### Custom<br/><br/>If you want to install on a fresh Ubuntu VM, use the a cloud images base qcow2 image.<br/><br/>curl -sSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/agentsea/agentd/main/remote_install.sh \| sudo bash<br/><br/>## API Endpoints<br/><br/>### General<br/><br/>* **GET /health** \- Checks the API's health.  <br/>   * **Response:** `\{"status": "ok"\}`<br/><br/>### Mouse and Keyboard Control<br/><br/>* **GET /mouse\_coordinates** \- Retrieves the current mouse coordinates.  <br/>   * **Response Model:** `CoordinatesModel`<br/>* **POST /move\_mouse** \- Moves the mouse to specified coordinates.  <br/>   * **Request Body:** `MoveMouseModel`  <br/>   * **Response:** `\{"status": "success"\}` or `\{"status": "error", "message": "<error_message>"\}`<br/>* **POST /click** \- Clicks at the current or specified location.  <br/>   * **Request Body:** `ClickModel`  <br/>   * **Response:** `\{"status": "success"\}` or raises `HTTPException`<br/>* **POST /double\_click** \- Performs a double-click at the current mouse location.  <br/>   * **Response:** `\{"status": "success"\}` or raises `HTTPException`<br/>* **POST /type\_text** \- Types the specified text.  <br/>   * **Request Body:** `TypeTextModel`  <br/>   * **Response:** `\{"status": "success"\}` or raises `HTTPException`<br/>* **POST /press\_key** \- Presses a specified key.  <br/>   * **Request Body:** `PressKeyModel`  <br/>   * **Response:** `\{"status": "success"\}` or raises `HTTPException`<br/>* **POST /scroll** \- Scrolls the mouse wheel.  <br/>   * **Request Body:** `ScrollModel`  <br/>   * **Response:** `\{"status": "success"\}` or raises `HTTPException`<br/>* **POST /drag\_mouse** \- Drags the mouse to specified coordinates.  <br/>   * **Request Body:** `DragMouseModel`  <br/>   * **Response:** `\{"status": "success"\}` or raises `HTTPException`<br/><br/>### Web Browser Control<br/><br/>* **POST /open\_url** \- Opens a URL in a Chromium-based browser.  <br/>   * **Request Body:** `OpenURLModel`  <br/>   * **Response:** `\{"status": "success"\}` or `\{"status": "error", "message": "<error_message>"\}`<br/><br/>### Screen Capture<br/><br/>* **POST /screenshot** \- Takes a screenshot and returns it as a base64-encoded image.  <br/>   * **Response Model:** `ScreenshotResponseModel`<br/><br/>### Session Recording<br/><br/>* **POST /recordings** \- Starts a new recording session.  <br/>   * **Request Body:** `RecordRequest`  <br/>   * **Response Model:** `RecordResponse`<br/>* **GET /recordings** \- Lists all recordings.  <br/>   * **Response Model:** `Recordings`<br/>* **POST /recordings/\{session\_id\}/stop** \- Stops a recording session.  <br/>   * **Path Variable:** `session_id`  <br/>   * **Response:** None (side effect: stops recording and saves to file)<br/>* **GET /recordings/\{session\_id\}** \- Retrieves information about a specific recording session.  <br/>   * **Path Variable:** `session_id`  <br/>   * **Response Model:** `Recording`<br/>* **GET /recordings/\{session\_id\}/event/\{event\_id\}** \- Retrieves a specific event from a recording.  <br/>   * **Path Variables:** `session_id`, `event_id`  <br/>   * **Response Model:** `RecordedEvent`<br/>* **DELETE /recordings/\{session\_id\}/event/\{event\_id\}** \- Deletes a specific event from a recording.  <br/>   * **Path Variables:** `session_id`, `event_id`  <br/>   * **Response Model:** `Recording`<br/>* **GET /active\_sessions** \- Lists IDs of all active recording sessions.  <br/>   * **Response Model:** `Recordings`<br/>* **GET /recordings/\{session\_id\}/actions** \- Retrieves all actions from a specific recording session.  <br/>   * **Path Variable:**
